title: Insurance Agencies and Brokerages

narrative.executive_view: Insurance agencies are unusually fertile for AI-enabled operating improvement but unusually unforgiving on purchase price. Service, quoting, policy checking, renewal preparation and commission reconciliation are automatable, while carrier-paid renewal commissions and commercial-line complexity help the operator retain savings [S2][S5]. The problem is that buyers already pay roughly low-double-digit EBITDA multiples for ordinary firms and about 14x for platforms [S8][S9]. This is therefore a durable operating thesis with weak conventional multiple arbitrage: attractive only when underwriting a commercial-heavy book, measurable service-labor removal and a price below headline market averages.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The near-term prize is not replacing licensed producers; it is shrinking the manual work around them. The 2026 ACT report identifies data entry, quote comparison, proposal assembly, policy checking, contract review and commission reconciliation as highly automatable, while keeping complex-risk judgment, carrier advocacy and client trust with humans [S2]. That supports high fractions for processing, clerical and customer-service roles, moderate fractions for producers, underwriters and adjusters, and a substantial but mixed residual. State producer licensing still requires a human or licensed entity to sell, solicit or negotiate insurance [S4], so the producer role changes toward review and advice rather than disappearing.
narrative.value_capture: Core agency revenue is paid by carriers as fixed percentages of premium on new and renewal policies, with contingent compensation tied to production, retention, growth and profitability [S5]. Because the customer does not usually buy agency labor by the hour, removing internal service cost need not immediately lower revenue; that makes distribution capture relatively strong. Leakage remains material: carriers control commission schedules, direct and embedded channels can bypass the agency, and buyers may pay away the benefit through elevated entry valuations. Licensing and human advice support terminal demand, but they are not counted twice as capture; the capture score rests on commission mechanics, renewal retention and the agency's ability to keep operating savings.
narrative.adoption_timing: AI interest is ahead of operational deployment. ACT reported that 68% of surveyed agencies were somewhat or very likely to increase AI use within twelve months, but only 8% were using it regularly and strategically; disconnected systems, missing processes, security and governance slow the move from experiments to effective workflow replacement [S2]. A three-year estimate to 50% effective adoption is therefore faster than legacy core-system replacement but slower than consumer-LLM use. Carrier APIs and AI features embedded in agency-management systems are the likely transmission mechanism, with commercial-data quality and compliance review setting the pace.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The market is already crowded. OPTIS counted 695 announced North American agent-and-broker deals in 2025; its seven most active named buyers completed between 22 and 69 deals each, and PE or hybrid buyers represented 73% of transactions [S7]. MarshBerry's 2024 database put the average upfront price for all firms at 11.22x EBITDA and platform firms at 14.02x; its 2026 discussion says private platforms have held near 14x base value while public broker medians fell toward 12x [S8][S9]. I therefore use 11.5x entry and 14.0x exit as transparent estimates, not promises. The spread is thin before integration cost, earnouts and leverage, and both benchmarks skew larger than a $1–10M EBITDA target.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand is durable for a commercial-heavy independent agency, though not invulnerable. The Big I reported that independent agencies placed 87.7% of U.S. commercial-lines premium and 62% of all P&C premium in 2025 [S3], and the ACT report says customers still strongly prefer agent help for purchase and policy management [S2]. Complex carrier appetites, renewals and licensed advice survive mature AI. Personal lines and standardized small-commercial risks face greater direct, embedded and AI-native pressure, so a buyer should underwrite the retained commercial mix rather than assume the entire six-digit code is equally protected.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are valuation, line-of-business mix and the gap between AI experimentation and actual headcount removal. The exact Census scope includes any establishment acting as agent or broker in selling annuities or insurance policies [S1], while the best technology and demographic surveys focus mainly on independent P&C agencies. The supplied occupation mix is at five digits and includes MGA or delegated-authority roles that may not appear in a retail target. The owner-age proxy is from a broad independent-agency survey, and headline M&A multiples mix roll-ins, stand-alone firms and platforms. Any deal should separately verify book retention, carrier concentration, producer ownership of relationships, normalized contingent commissions and whether quoted EBITDA already reflects outsourced service labor.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition — 524210 Insurance Agencies and Brokerages
sources.what_it_supports: Exact six-digit scope: establishments acting as agents or brokers in selling annuities and insurance policies, excluding carriers and title-settlement offices.

sources.title: 2026 Big I Agents Council for Technology Tech Trends Report
sources.what_it_supports: Agency AI adoption, implementation barriers, automatable service and back-office tasks, durable producer functions, and customer preference for agent assistance.

sources.title: Big I Releases 2026 Market Share Report
sources.what_it_supports: Independent agencies placed 62% of U.S. P&C premium and 87.7% of commercial-lines premium in 2025.

sources.title: NAIC Producer Licensing
sources.what_it_supports: State regulators license people who sell, solicit or negotiate insurance; licensing is a real but nonexclusive channel constraint.

sources.title: The Hanover Producer Compensation Disclosure
sources.what_it_supports: Carrier-paid base commissions are generally fixed percentages of premium on new and renewal policies, with additional compensation tied to production, retention, growth and profitability.

sources.title: 2023 Agency Growth Study: Agencies Anticipate Ownership Changes
sources.what_it_supports: Among more than 450 surveyed agency principals and owners, 40% were older than 60; recruiting talent and ownership transition were documented challenges.

sources.title: OPTIS Partners North American Agent & Broker 2025 Year-End M&A Report
sources.what_it_supports: 695 announced 2025 deals, 73% PE/hybrid share, and seven named highly active buyers with 22–69 deals each.

sources.title: Clock Speed and the Landscape of Risk
sources.what_it_supports: In 2026, public broker median valuation was about 12x EBITDA and private platform firms had averaged about 14x base purchase price and about 19x with earnout over three years.

sources.title: MarshBerry 2024 M&A Year in Review
sources.what_it_supports: 2024 all-firm upfront purchase prices averaged 11.22x EBITDA and platform firms averaged 14.02x; maximum earnout values were higher.

sources.title: Big I and Future One Release 2024 Agency Universe Findings
sources.what_it_supports: Independent P&C agency population, ownership-change expectations, and adoption of digital agency workflows such as e-signature and commission statements.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524210 ($103,879,237,202) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524210 ($207,109,590,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists

n_band.derivation: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $256,440 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524210); class <5: 91,066 firms, avg revenue/firm $323,793 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $76,318 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $929,879 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $219,173 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 5,672 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,313,385 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $545,265 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,689 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,126,954 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,523 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 547 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,097,678 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,451,023 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 379 firms, avg revenue/firm $269,239,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,459,770 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,236

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Task-level judgment applies ACT's documented automation targets to every supplied SOC while reserving licensed advice, relationship selling, complex placement and accountable review for humans. The residual is treated as mixed account-management, finance, marketing and technical work rather than assumed fully automatable.
caveats: The 52421 occupation mix includes MGAs, wholesale brokers and delegated-authority work as well as retail agencies; target books can differ materially.
Fractions describe technically replaceable tasks at mature deployment, not current adoption or immediate headcount reduction.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Quoting, comparison, submission and proposal work automate, but licensed advice, complex placement and relationship selling remain.
Routine requests, certificates, endorsements, renewal preparation and status communication are workflow-heavy; escalations and advocacy remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Policy processing is document extraction, checking, routing and system entry, with humans retained for exceptions and accountability.
Reporting, analytics and routine coordination automate; carrier negotiation, hiring, compliance ownership and operating judgment persist.
Routine desk review and file synthesis automate, while field facts, negotiation and contested judgment remain human.
Scheduling, document handling, data entry and standard correspondence are highly automatable; physical and exception tasks remain.
Risk triage, file assembly and appetite matching automate; delegated authority, judgment and carrier accountability persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Queue management, quality sampling and reporting automate, while coaching, exception escalation and compliance supervision remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Calendar, correspondence, document preparation and routine coordination are mature automation targets.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Content, segmentation, campaign preparation and first-pass analysis automate, but positioning and compliance review remain.
The large unlisted tail likely mixes account managers, finance, IT, compliance, marketing and specialty roles; automation is substantial but heterogeneous.

pi_dist.rationale: Carrier-paid percentage commissions and renewals make client price less directly responsive to agency service cost, so an agency can initially retain most internal labor savings. Carrier schedule cuts, producer compensation and direct-channel competition create leakage.
caveats: Carrier commission schedules and contingent income are controlled upstream and can change; fee-based large-commercial accounts expose cost more directly.

pi_moat.rationale: Renewal relationships, carrier access and complex commercial advice support retention, but producer books can be portable and carriers or embedded distributors can go direct. Licensing protects accountable activity, not the independent-agency channel itself.
caveats: Commercial-heavy agencies have more moat than personal-lines and standardized small-commercial books; licensing is not treated as an exclusive channel franchise.

t50_years.rationale: Only 8% reported regular strategic AI use despite strong planned increases. Embedded AMS tools and carrier APIs should accelerate deployment, but workflow standardization, security, governance and legacy interfaces make 50% effective use a multi-year transition.
caveats: The survey measures agency-reported usage rather than verified labor substitution, and target sophistication varies sharply by size.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: ACT reported that 8% of surveyed independent agencies were currently using AI regularly and strategically, the closest available measure of effective rather than experimental use.
current_adoption_pct.plausible_range: 0.08 observed strategic use; broader experimentation is much higher
caveats: National Big I member survey; regular strategic use is a stricter definition than any AI use and does not prove headcount removal.

historical_analogs.value: Agency e-signature and direct-bill workflow adoption followed feature embedding in carrier and management systems; AI is likely to diffuse similarly, but with higher governance and data-integration requirements.
historical_analogs.rationale: The 2024 Agency Universe findings show broad uptake of narrower digital workflows, while the 2026 ACT report shows AI still moving from experiments into core systems. The analog supports steady embedded adoption, not overnight autonomous operation.
caveats: E-signature is a simpler, lower-liability tool than AI-assisted policy advice or autonomous workflow execution.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: The 2023 Agency Growth Study reported 40% of surveyed independent-agency principals older than 60.
caveats: More than 450 principals and owners were surveyed; it is not a Census estimate and may not match the $1–10M EBITDA cohort.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The studies document imminent ownership changes, perpetuation challenges and recruiting or leadership-pipeline constraints.
caveats: A documented channel-wide challenge does not mean every target lacks an internal successor.

active_consolidators.rationale: OPTIS explicitly named seven highly active 2025 buyers—BroadStreet, Hub, Inszone, World, Keystone, ALKEME and King Risk—with 22 to 69 announced deals each.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 7 named leaders; dozens of additional active buyers
caveats: North American announced-deal data covers insurance distribution broadly and includes transactions outside exact NAICS 524210 or the target EBITDA band.

buy_mult.rationale: The estimate rounds the 2024 all-firm upfront average of 11.22x upward to reflect still-elevated 2026 pricing, while remaining below the approximately 14x private-platform benchmark.
caveats: MarshBerry mixes roll-ins, stand-alone firms and platforms and skews larger than many $1–10M EBITDA targets; earnouts and contingent consideration are excluded.

exit_mult.rationale: A scaled, diversified platform is benchmarked to the roughly 14x private-platform base multiple reported both for 2024 and the subsequent three-year period, without credit for maximum earnouts.
caveats: Exit requires genuine platform scale, organic growth, retention and infrastructure; a collection of small books may still price as a roll-in, and 2026 public comparables had fallen toward 12x.

pricing_structure.value: Carrier-paid recurring commission as a percentage of policy premium, supplemented by contingent commissions and occasional client fees
pricing_structure.rationale: Carrier disclosure directly documents base commissions on new and renewal premium plus incentive compensation; exact schedules vary by carrier, line, state and producer.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Predominantly carrier commissions; fee mix rises in some large-commercial and specialty accounts
caveats: The Hanover is one carrier example and does not establish a universal commission percentage.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The modeled replaceable share applies to mature task capability; three years to 50% effective adoption and a longer path to full realization prevent treating the full uplift as immediate.

terminal_value.justification: Commercial-risk complexity, renewals, multi-carrier access and licensed accountable advice sustain paid brokerage demand, though personal and standardized small-commercial distribution face direct and embedded pressure.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: The Census source establishes exact 524210 scope. P&C agency surveys, one carrier compensation disclosure, five-digit OEWS roles and broad broker M&A databases are disclosed proxies rather than silently treated as exact six-digit evidence.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Agency labor savings are not directly repriced to clients under carrier-paid commission schedules, but carriers can trim schedules and direct or embedded channels can compress the commission pool over time.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied occupation mix is 5-digit 52421 rather than exact 524210 and blends retail agencies with wholesale, MGA and delegated-authority work; the source surveys emphasize independent P&C agencies.
sensitivity: A retail personal-lines book has less underwriting and claims work and more direct-channel exposure than the supplied mix.
An MGA-heavy target can have more underwriter labor and different carrier economics, changing both replaceability and capture.
Benefits, life and annuity agencies have distinct commission, regulation and technology patterns.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Retail independent P&C and benefits agencies, with a commercial-heavy target preferred
market_boundary.selection_basis: The targetable firm population, available agency-specific surveys and recurring commission economics are most representative of independent retail distribution; direct carriers, claims administrators and title agencies are outside the exact code.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A two-year outcome materially raises adoption score; a five-year integration cycle lowers it.
Entry price determines whether any platform exit spread exists after integration and financing cost.
Failure to earn platform quality can eliminate the modeled multiple spread entirely.
Personal-lines mix and producer portability can materially reduce retained savings and terminal resilience.

reviewer_note: Exact NAICS scope and both EBITDA multiple assumptions are explicitly sourced; multiple evidence is broader and larger-company-biased, so entry and exit are estimates with size and transaction-structure caveats.
